verbatim_text: In this case, when you have let's say 1 million or 10 million context, it's rather imagined. So, for this phase of my research, I'd like to first explore how, let's suppose we want to go for the cleanest case of one model per user. Does this actually become practical? And later, I think to expand the practicality of this general approach, yes, users that have similar queries, they use similar context, let's say people in the same class or in the same company, whatever, they should share a model. You repeatedly said that classical setup don't change the weights during test time. Let me push back on this. I think that's true. So, for LLMs, OpenAI doesn't change their weights just because I type something in. It would be a terrible idea to do. Same with self-driving cars. You could teach the car something weird. But there is of course online learning. It's the main adaptation, which are basically settings where during test time, you react to the labels you get. So, can you maybe address that a little bit?
